title: How the Coup Turned Myanmar Into Asia's Deadliest Conflict | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Myanmar's 2021 coup reversed a decade of 6%-a-year growth into Asia's deadliest conflict (100,000+ dead, poverty back to ~50%); the junta turned the tide militarily via an authoritarian-collaboration network (Russia, Belarus, Iran, China) and is now buying US respectability through Trump-connected lobbyists as sanctions are quietly lifted and aid collapses.

themes.theme: Economic reversal from opening to collapse

insights.claim: In Myanmar's 2011-2020 opening the economy grew about 6 percent a year and poverty fell from 48.2 percent in 2005 to 24.8 percent by 2017, with FDI peaking near $9.5 billion in 2015 and standing at ~$4.9 billion in the last full pre-coup fiscal year (2019-2020).
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Baseline for what was destroyed—quantifies the frontier-market opportunity the coup erased and the scale of any future reconstruction upside.
insights.locator: It was not always this way.

insights.claim: After the February 2021 coup Myanmar's GDP shrank nearly 18 percent in 2021 and barely recovered, poverty roughly doubled to 49.7 percent by 2023 erasing more than a decade of gains, the middle class was cut in half, inflation topped 30 percent, and foreign investment collapsed nearly three-quarters.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Documents a near-total economic reversal—Myanmar is effectively uninvestable, with a shrunken consumer base and collapsed capital inflows.

insights.claim: The UN counts 15.2 million people—nearly a third of Myanmar's population—facing acute food insecurity, with Rakhine State on the edge of famine, 3.5 million people displaced, and the health system all but collapsed after medical facilities were damaged more than 330 times.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Humanitarian collapse of this scale signals prolonged instability and refugee outflows pressuring neighboring economies.
insights.locator: Skilled workers have fled by the hundreds of thousands

insights.claim: Myanmar overtook Afghanistan in 2023 as the world's largest opium producer at an estimated 1,080 metric tons worth up to $2.4 billion, and became the planet's methamphetamine engine—Thailand alone seized a record one billion meth tablets in 2024 that nearly all came from Myanmar.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: A failed-state narco/meth hub of this scale is a durable regional security and cross-border crime externality for Southeast Asia.

insights.claim: Cyber scam compounds inside Myanmar, often staffed by trafficked workers held in near-slavery, generate billions of dollars a year, and one global index now ranks Myanmar the most crime-ridden country on earth.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Industrial-scale cyber-scam operations are a rising cross-border fraud and financial-crime vector with global victim exposure.

themes.theme: How the junta turned the tide: authoritarian collaboration

insights.claim: By late 2024 an alliance of ethnic armies and volunteer militias controlled roughly half of Myanmar and pushed to within fourteen miles of Mandalay, making it look as though the generals could lose the civil war.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Establishes that the junta's survival was not inevitable—external resupply, not military skill, reversed a losing position.
insights.locator: Despite the viciousness of the military junta's campaign

insights.claim: What turned the tide was an authoritarian-collaboration network: Belarus supplied V3D radar and ground-based missile systems, Myanmar in 2025 became the first buyer of Russia's new assault transport helicopters, Iran secretly supplied critical jet fuel, and Beijing dangled some $3 billion in assistance.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of a functioning dictator-support network—implies sanctions alone cannot topple regimes backed by this axis.
insights.locator: What turned the tide was not the generals

insights.claim: The junta's signature weapon is the airstrike against civilians, with junta airstrikes nearly doubling in a single year—from 2,471 in 2024 to 4,881 in 2025—and ACLED attributing roughly 71 percent of civilian fatalities to the junta.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Air dominance restored by foreign hardware is the mechanism of the junta's comeback—tracking jet-fuel and helicopter supply is the key leading indicator.
insights.locator: And then there is the killing.

themes.theme: International abandonment and US drift toward the regime

insights.claim: ASEAN's Five-Point Consensus, agreed days after the coup, was repudiated by the junta within two days and flouted ever since, underscoring the regional bloc's inability to constrain the regime.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: ASEAN provides no effective check—do not expect regional diplomacy to alter Myanmar's trajectory.
insights.locator: And still the world looks away.

insights.claim: The UN's 2025 humanitarian plan for Myanmar—$1.14 billion to reach nearly 20 million people—was only 12 percent funded by midyear, and the Trump administration's dismantling of USAID axed an estimated $259 million in Myanmar aid in 2025 alone.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Aid collapse removes a humanitarian backstop and signals reduced Western engagement, deepening the crisis and refugee pressure.
insights.locator: Aid for the country is dwindling too.

insights.claim: The US is quietly drifting toward the regime—in July 2025 the Treasury lifted sanctions on several companies and individuals linked to the Myanmar military, including men accused of brokering its weapons.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Sanctions relief signals a potential normalization path—a regime-risk repricing for any entities with junta ties or exposure.
insights.locator: More telling still is the United States' quiet drift

insights.claim: The junta has spent lavishly to court Washington, hiring the Trump-connected firm DCI Group on a $3 million contract with longtime Trump confidant Roger Stone drawing $50,000 a month to 'rebuild' relations.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Shows a pay-for-access lobbying channel to the Trump administration—a mechanism by which pariah regimes may buy sanctions relief.
insights.quote: paying with the blood of its people
insights.locator: More telling still is the United States' quiet drift
